summary: Re-established in 1867 on the San Antonio–El Paso Road, Fort Davis became one of the largest frontier posts where Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry garrisoned, patrolled, and built infrastructure across West Texas until the 1890s. The restored post is a National Park Service unit documenting African American service on the frontier.
locationLabel: Fort Davis National Historic Site, 101 Lt. Flipper Drive, Fort Davis

claims.object: After the Civil War, Fort Davis was reoccupied in 1867 and became a major frontier post where Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry served through the 1880s, protecting travel on the San Antonio–El Paso Road
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: African Americans in the Frontier Army, Fort Davis National Historic Site

claims.object: Fort Davis National Historic Site preserves one of the best surviving examples of a frontier military post in the Southwest, with restored and ruin buildings documenting Army life on the Texas frontier
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Davis National Historic Site

historicalContext: Buffalo Soldier units at Fort Davis mapped passes, escorted mail and wagon trains, and pursued raiders across the Trans-Pecos. Second Lieutenant Henry O. Flipper, the first Black West Point graduate, served at the post before his controversial court-martial — a story told today alongside the broader record of Black regulars on the frontier.

summary: Established in 1877 in southeastern Arizona, Fort Huachuca became the headquarters of the 10th Cavalry and a long-term home for Buffalo Soldier regiments that patrolled the borderlands, fought in the Apache campaigns, and later served through two world wars. Its historic district and museum document more than a century of Black military service in the West.
locationLabel: Fort Huachuca Old Post, 41401 Grierson Avenue, Fort Huachuca

claims.object: Fort Huachuca served as headquarters of the 10th U.S. Cavalry and hosted Buffalo Soldier units including the 9th Cavalry, 24th Infantry, and 25th Infantry across the Apache Wars and border-service eras
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Huachuca

claims.object: The Fort Huachuca Historic District was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1974, recognizing the post's role in frontier Army history including Buffalo Soldier service
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: National Register of Historic Places

historicalContext: From the 1870s through the early twentieth century, Black troopers at Huachuca mapped trails, built telegraph lines, and fought in campaigns against Apache leaders including Geronimo. The post remains an active U.S. Army installation and houses the Fort Huachuca Museum on its historic Old Post.

summary: Built in 1867 at the junction of the North and Middle Concho Rivers in San Angelo, Fort Concho anchored U.S. control of West Texas. Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry garrisoned the post from 1869 to 1885, scouting, building roads, and protecting settlers on the frontier.
locationLabel: Fort Concho National Historic Landmark, 630 South Oakes Street, San Angelo

claims.object: Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry served at Fort Concho from 1869 to 1885, among the longest continuous Black garrisons on a single Texas frontier post
claims.citationLabel: Fort Concho National Historic Landmark: Buffalo Soldiers

claims.object: Fort Concho was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1961 for its exceptionally well-preserved frontier military architecture and documentation of post–Civil War western expansion
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Concho

historicalContext: San Angelo's fort was a supply hub for campaigns across the Llano Estacado. Black troopers here faced harsh desert duty and earned the respect — and the nickname — that frontier peoples applied to the segregated regiments across the West.

summary: Built in 1858 in the San Luis Valley, Fort Garland guarded settlers in southern Colorado. From 1876 to 1879 the 9th Cavalry — Buffalo Soldiers — garrisoned the post under Colonel Kit Carson's successor command structure, patrolling Ute country before the fort closed in 1883.
locationLabel: Fort Garland Museum, 29477 Highway 159, Fort Garland

claims.object: Company G of the 9th U.S. Cavalry, a Buffalo Soldier unit, garrisoned Fort Garland from 1876 to 1879, replacing white troops withdrawn from the San Luis Valley
claims.citationLabel: History Colorado: Fort Garland Museum & Pike's Stockade

claims.object: Fort Garland was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1970; five of its original adobe buildings survive as a Colorado History Museum site
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Garland

historicalContext: The San Luis Valley assignment placed Black cavalrymen between Ute communities and incoming Anglo and Hispanic settlers during a volatile period of treaty breakdown. Fort Garland today interprets both the Buffalo Soldier era and the broader military occupation of Colorado's mountain frontier.

summary: Established in 1865 on the Rio Grande near Las Cruces, Fort Selden housed Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry who protected settlers, mail routes, and railroad construction in southern New Mexico. Ruins of the adobe post are preserved as a New Mexico Historic Site.
locationLabel: Fort Selden Historic Site, 1280 Fort Selden Road, Radium Springs

claims.object: Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry served at Fort Selden from the late 1860s through the 1880s, guarding the Rio Grande valley and southern transcontinental routes
claims.citationLabel: New Mexico Historic Sites: Fort Selden

claims.object: Fort Selden Historic Site preserves adobe ruins and interprets frontier Army life, including a dedicated focus on Buffalo Soldier service in the Mesilla Valley
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Selden

historicalContext: Among the children who lived at Fort Selden during the Buffalo Soldier era was Douglas MacArthur, whose father commanded the post — a reminder that Black regulars served alongside and under white officers across the Southwestern garrison network.

summary: Built between 1919 and 1923 near the Arizona–Sonora border, Camp Naco is the last surviving adobe post from the U.S. Army's Mexican Border Defense network. Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 25th Infantry garrisoned the cantonment from 1911 to 1924, patrolling a border crossed daily by revolution-era violence.
locationLabel: Camp Naco Historic District, 2118 West Newell Street, Naco

claims.object: African American troops from the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 25th Infantry — Buffalo Soldiers — were assigned to Camp Naco from 1911 to 1924 to patrol the border during the Mexican Revolution
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Camp Naco

claims.object: The Camp Naco Historic District was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 2012 as the only intact adobe border-defense camp from the 1919–1923 building program
claims.citationLabel: National Register of Historic Places: Camp Naco Historic District (12000853)

historicalContext: Camp Naco's Buffalo Soldiers watched battles of the Mexican Revolution from the American side of the line while protecting railroads and towns. Community-led stabilization efforts continue to preserve the adobe officers' quarters and enlisted men's barracks.

summary: Fort Craig guarded the Rio Grande crossing on the El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro south of Socorro. After the Civil War, Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 38th and 125th Infantry rotated through the post, patrolling Apache country until the fort was abandoned in 1885.
locationLabel: Fort Craig National Historic Site, 15700 Highway 1, San Antonio, New Mexico

claims.object: Following the Civil War Battle of Valverde fought at the post, Fort Craig was re-garrisoned by Buffalo Soldiers including units of the 9th Cavalry who patrolled southern New Mexico through the 1870s and 1880s
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Craig

claims.object: Fort Craig National Historic Site is managed by the Bureau of Land Management and preserves ruins of one of the largest frontier forts in the Southwest
claims.citationLabel: Bureau of Land Management: Fort Craig National Historic Site

historicalContext: The fort's Civil War earthworks and adobe ruins sit along a trade route older than the Army itself. Buffalo Soldier patrols from Fort Craig linked a chain of posts — Selden, McRae, and Stanton — that controlled movement through Apache homelands.

summary: Founded in 1903 on Creek Nation land in Okfuskee County, Boley grew from a railroad construction camp into one of the largest and most prosperous all-Black towns in the United States, with its own bank, schools, and businesses. Its surviving commercial core is a National Historic Landmark District.
locationLabel: Boley Historic District, bounded by Seward Avenue, Cedar Street, Walnut Street, and southern city limits

claims.object: The Boley Historic District was listed in the National Register of Historic Places and designated a National Historic Landmark in 1975, recognizing one of the nation's most significant surviving all-Black towns
claims.citationLabel: National Register of Historic Places: Boley Historic District (75001568)

claims.object: Boley was founded in 1903 on land allotted to Creek Freedmen, growing from a Black railroad construction camp into a self-governing town of several thousand residents by the 1910s
claims.citationLabel: The Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture: Boley

historicalContext: Booker T. Washington visited Boley and praised it as proof that Black towns could thrive in the West. The annual Boley Rodeo, begun in the early twentieth century, remains a living tradition of the town's ranching culture.

summary: Opened for settlement in April 1890 in Oklahoma Territory, Langston was promoted by Black town builders including Edward P. McCabe as a self-governing community where African Americans could own land and vote. Langston University, established in 1897, helped the town survive as one of Oklahoma's thirteen remaining historic all-Black towns.

claims.object: Langston was opened for settlement on April 22, 1890, by promoters including Edward P. McCabe and landowner Charles Robbins, who marketed the town to Black migrants from Kansas and the South
claims.citationLabel: The Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture: Langston

claims.object: The Oklahoma Territorial Legislature established the Colored Agricultural and Normal University at Langston in 1897 — now Langston University, Oklahoma's only public HBCU
claims.citationLabel: City of Langston: About Us

historicalContext: Unlike All-Black towns founded in Indian Territory, Langston and nearby Brooksville were planted in Oklahoma Territory during the land-run era. McCabe's Langston City Herald urged migrants to build political power through concentrated settlement — a western counterpart to Exoduster colonization in Kansas.

summary: Founded in 1910 by Oliver Toussaint Jackson on the dry plains of Weld County, Dearfield became the largest Black homesteading settlement in Colorado, reaching roughly 300 residents and profitable dryland farms before drought and the Dust Bowl emptied the town. Two buildings and the townsite survive as a National Register historic district.
locationLabel: Dearfield Historic District, along Colorado Highway 34 west of Wiggins

claims.object: The Dearfield historic district was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1995, preserving the founder's filling station, residence, and townsite ruins of Colorado's largest Black homesteading colony
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Dearfield, Colorado

claims.object: Oliver Toussaint Jackson founded Dearfield in 1910 through the Negro Townsite and Land Company, drawing nineteen initial homesteaders who built dugouts and later frame houses on the high plains northeast of Denver
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Dearfield Special Resource Study

historicalContext: Dearfield's prosperity during World War I — when homesteaders netted more than $50,000 from a single harvest — showed that Black farmers could succeed on Colorado's arid land before climate and markets turned. The Black American West Museum in Denver holds preservation efforts at the site.

summary: Incorporated in 1903 by Frank and Ella Boyer and twelve other African American founders, Blackdom was the first all-Black settlement in New Mexico, peaking at roughly 300 residents with a church, school, post office, and store on the Pecos Valley prairie south of Roswell before drought drove families out by the mid-1920s.
locationLabel: Blackdom Townsite historical marker, near Hagerman, Chaves County

claims.object: The Blackdom Townsite Company was incorporated in 1903 with Frank Boyer as president; settlement began around 1908–1909 when homesteaders filed claims under the Homestead Act and related land laws
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Blackdom, New Mexico

claims.object: The New Mexico Historic Preservation Division erected a state historical marker at the Blackdom townsite commemorating the community founded by Francis Marion and Ella Boyer
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Blackdom Townsite

historicalContext: Frank Boyer walked from Georgia to New Mexico carrying his father's stories of free land in the West. Blackdom's street names — Washington, Lincoln, and Love — mapped political aspirations onto desert homesteads at a time when Roswell's growing white population offered Black settlers few alternatives.

summary: In 1879 Exodusters — many from Tennessee — purchased land in Topeka's King's Addition and built Tennessee Town, one of the first concentrated Black neighborhoods in Kansas's capital and a western anchor of the Exoduster migration. By the 1890s the community supported churches, schools, and the first Black kindergarten west of the Mississippi River.
locationLabel: Tennessee Town neighborhood, bounded by SW Munson Avenue, SW Clay Street, SW Huntoon Street, and SW Washburn Avenue

claims.object: In 1879 a colony of approximately 500 Exodusters purchased land in Topeka's King's Addition; many migrants came from Tennessee, giving the neighborhood its name
claims.citationLabel: Kansas Historical Society: Tennessee Town Survey

claims.object: In 1893 Rev. Charles Sheldon opened the Tennessee Town Kindergarten in Central Congregational Church — documented as the first Black kindergarten west of the Mississippi River
claims.citationLabel: Topeka & Shawnee County Public Library: Exodusters in Topeka

historicalContext: Topeka was the only Kansas city with an organized Exoduster relief program through the Kansas Freedmen's Relief Association. Skilled Exoduster carpenters and masons helped build the expanding state capitol while Tennessee Town's churches anchored community life a mile southwest of the dome.

displayName: Bill Pickett Memorial, Fort Worth Stockyards
summary: Born in 1870 in Texas's Williamson County, Bill Pickett invented bulldogging — wrestling steers by the lip — and became one of the most famous Black cowboys in the West, performing with the 101 Ranch Wild West Show. A bronze memorial at the Fort Worth Stockyards honors his legacy in the cattle-driving culture Black Texans helped build.
locationLabel: Bill Pickett Memorial, Fort Worth Stockyards National Historic District, East Exchange Avenue

claims.object: A Bill Pickett memorial stands in the Fort Worth Stockyards National Historic District, commemorating the Black cowboy who invented bulldogging and performed nationally with the 101 Ranch Wild West Show
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Fort Worth Stockyards

claims.object: Bill Pickett was the first Black cowboy inducted into the Rodeo Hall of Fame at the National Cowboy & Western Heritage Museum in 1971, decades after his death in 1932
claims.citationLabel: National Cowboy & Western Heritage Museum: Bill Pickett

historicalContext: Historians estimate that one in four cowboys in the late nineteenth-century cattle trade was Black, though rodeo and Hollywood later erased that presence. Pickett's bulldogging technique — biting a steer's lip to bring it down — made him a star but also underscored the raw skill Black horsemen brought to the range.

summary: In 1908 the Black cowboy and ranch foreman George McJunkin discovered ancient bison bones in a arroyo after a flood on Crowfoot Ranch near Folsom, New Mexico — evidence that led archaeologists to the 1926 Folsom excavation proving human presence in North America more than 10,000 years ago. The Folsom Site is listed in the National Register of Historic Places.
locationLabel: Folsom Site, Wild Horse Arroyo, near Folsom, Union County

claims.object: George McJunkin, a formerly enslaved Black cowboy and ranch foreman at Crowfoot Ranch, found deeply buried bison bones exposed by a 1908 flood in Wild Horse Arroyo and recognized their antiquity
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Folsom Site

claims.object: The Folsom Site was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1978 after the 1926 excavation confirmed Folsom projectile points alongside extinct bison, rewriting the timeline of human settlement in the Americas
claims.citationLabel: National Register of Historic Places: Folsom Site (78001416)

historicalContext: McJunkin spent years trying to interest scientists in the bones he found while working cattle on the High Plains. His discovery did not bring him recognition in his lifetime, but the Folsom Site now stands as a landmark where a Black cowboy's eye for the land changed American archaeology.

summary: Buffalo Soldier regiments garrisoned Fort Bliss and the El Paso borderlands from 1869 to 1885, protecting routes through the Southwest. The 1999 bronze memorial titled The Errand of Corporal Ross, depicting 9th Cavalry trooper John Ross, stands at the post's Buffalo Soldier Road entrance.
locationLabel: Buffalo Soldier Memorial, Buffalo Soldier Road, Fort Bliss

claims.object: Several Buffalo Soldier regiments, including the 9th and 10th Cavalry, garrisoned Fort Bliss and nearby areas from 1869 to 1885 during the Army's westward expansion
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Army NCO Journal: A Belated Tribute to the Buffalo Soldiers at Fort Bliss

claims.object: The Errand of Corporal Ross, a bronze Buffalo Soldier memorial by artist Bob Snead, was erected at Fort Bliss in 1999 along what was renamed Buffalo Soldier Road in 2014
claims.citationLabel: Texas Time Travel: Buffalo Soldier Memorial Fort Bliss

historicalContext: Buffalo Soldier units produced twenty-three Medal of Honor recipients during the Indian Wars and later conflicts. Fifty-two Buffalo Soldiers are buried at Fort Bliss National Cemetery and nearby Concordia Cemetery in El Paso.
